en.extract:
The Puerto Rican tanager (Nesospingus speculiferus) is a small passerine bird endemic to the archipelago of Puerto Rico. It is the only member of the genus Nesospingus and has historically been placed in the tanager family, but recent studies indicate it as either belonging in its own family Nesospingidae or as being a member of Phaenicophilidae. Its closest relatives are likely the spindalises (family Spindalidae, sometimes also considered a member of the Phaenicophilidae). The Puerto Rican tanager is known to locals as llorosa, which means 'cryer'.


Description


Identification
The Puerto Rican tanager is a small passerine, typically measuring between 18 and 20 cm (7 and 8 in) in length and weighing around 36 g (1.3 oz). Both males and females are olive-brown above with pale grey to white underparts. Adults typically have faint dusky striping on the breast and pure white throats. Adults also have a conspicuous white spot on the wing and a dark crown and face which obscures the eye. Undertail coverts are pale fulvous. Males have a brown-black maxilla and white mandible and females have entirely black bills. Immatures are similar in appearance to adults, but are brownish underneath and lack the white wing spot.


Voice
The most frequently heard noise emitted by the Puerto Rican tanager is a harsh call note often described as a chewp or chuck. This is often heard while feeding in flocks and may be extended into a longer chi-chi-chit of varying lengths. The breeding song of the species is light, sweet rapidly sung tswet-tswet-tswet-tswet. Other interaction calls include a soft sigh similar to a heavy exhale and a light tsip-tsip-tsip.


Behavior
Puerto Rican tanagers are known to roost communally in large bamboo clumps or palms. They are typically the nucleus species in mixed feeding flocks, especially in the winter when neotropical migrants are present in Puerto Rico. During the breeding season, tanagers become very territorial and defend nesting territories. Puerto Rican tanagers are strong flyers, but don't often fly long distances, preferring to make short flights through the canopy or brush.


Feeding
Puerto Rican tanagers feed mainly on invertebrates and fruits. The species has been reported to occasionally consume lizards and the nestling of other birds, but most of its diet consists of spiders, insects, centipedes, snails, and various fruits. Fruit consumption is determined by season, but they often feed on fruits of the genus Cecropia and Clusia. Eleutherodactylus tree frogs, such as the common coquí, are also an important component of the Puerto Rican tanager's diet.


Breeding

The breeding season lasts from January to late-July, though individuals have been recorded breeding at other times of the year. During this time the males become strongly territorial. Nests are located at the ends of branches 2–10 m (10–30 ft) off the ground and are usually cup-shaped and approximately 9.2 cm (3.6 in) across. They are typically constructed of vines, ferns, roots, and palm fibers and lined with feathers and palm leaves. Females have a clutch of two or three elliptical white eggs with reddish-brown splotches.


Predators
Communal roosting makes the species an easy target for owls on the island, such as the Puerto Rican owl. They are also a common prey item of the Puerto Rican sharp-shinned hawk.


Distribution and habitat
The Puerto Rican tanager is restricted to mid- to high-elevation (300–1,350 m (1,000–4,400 ft) montane forests on the island of Puerto Rico. It typically inhabits mature and second growth montane subtropical rain and wet forests, as well as subtropical lower montane forests. Much of the population is concentrated on the eastern and western sides of the central cordillera of Puerto Rico, with populations in El Yunque National Forest and Maricao State Forest. Deforestation has contributed to fragmentation of the population, which once stretched across the entire central cordillera, but is now confined to the preserved areas and higher peaks.


References

The '''Puerto Rican tanager''' ('''''Nesospingus speculiferus''''') is a small [[passerine]] bird [[Endemic (ecology)|endemic]] to the [[archipelago]] of [[Puerto Rico]]. It is the [[Monotypic taxon|only member]] of the genus '''''Nesospingus''''' and has historically been placed in the [[tanager]] family, but recent studies indicate it as either belonging in its own family '''Nesospingidae''' or as being a member of [[Phaenicophilidae]]. Its closest relatives are likely the [[Spindalis|spindalises]] (family [[Spindalis|Spindalidae]], sometimes also considered a member of the Phaenicophilidae).<ref name="oliveros">{{cite journal|last1=Oliveros|first1=C.H.|display-authors=etal|year=2019|title=Earth history and the passerine superradiation|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States|volume=116|issue=16|pages=7916–7925|doi=10.1073/pnas.1813206116|pmid=30936315|pmc=6475423|bibcode=2019PNAS..116.7916O |doi-access=free}}</ref> The Puerto Rican tanager is known to locals as {{Lang|es|llorosa}}, which means 'cryer'.

== Description ==

=== Identification ===
The Puerto Rican tanager is a small passerine, typically measuring between {{cvt|18|and|20|cm|in|0}} in length and weighing around {{cvt|36|g}}. Both males and females are olive-brown above with pale grey to white underparts. Adults typically have faint dusky striping on the breast and pure white throats. Adults also have a conspicuous white spot on the wing and a dark crown and face which obscures the eye.<ref name=":0">{{cite book|author=Raffaele, H.|author2= J. Wiley|author3= O. Garrido|author4= A. Keith|author5= J. Raffaele |year= 1998|title= A guide to the birds of the West Indies|publisher= Princeton University Press|location= Princeton, New Jersey}}</ref> Undertail coverts are pale fulvous. Males have a brown-black [[maxilla]] and white [[mandible]] and females have entirely black bills. Immatures are similar in appearance to adults, but are brownish underneath and lack the white wing spot.

=== Voice ===
The most frequently heard noise emitted by the Puerto Rican tanager is a harsh call note often described as a ''{{Not a typo|chewp}}'' or ''{{Not a typo|chuck}}''. This is often heard while feeding in flocks and may be extended into a longer ''{{Not a typo|chi-chi-chit}}'' of varying lengths. The breeding song of the species is light, sweet rapidly sung ''{{Not a typo|tswet-tswet-tswet-tswet}}''. Other interaction calls include a soft ''sigh'' similar to a heavy exhale and a light ''{{Not a typo|tsip-tsip-tsip}}''.<ref name=":0" />

== Behavior ==
Puerto Rican tanagers are known to roost communally in large bamboo clumps or palms.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{cite journal|author=Wetmore, A. |year=1927|title= The Birds of Porto Rico and the Virgin Islands|journal= New York Academy of Sciences Scientific Survey of Porto Rico and Virgin Islands |volume=9|issue=3&4|pages= 254–406}}</ref> They are typically the nucleus species in mixed feeding flocks, especially in the winter when neotropical migrants are present in Puerto Rico. During the breeding season, tanagers become very territorial and defend nesting territories.<ref name=":1">{{cite journal|author=Pérez-Rivera, R. A. |year=1993|title= Notes on the Breeding of the Puerto Rican Tanager (''Nesospingus speculiferus'')|journal= Caribbean Journal of Science |volume=29|issue=3–4|pages= 262–264}}</ref> Puerto Rican tanagers are strong flyers, but don't often fly long distances, preferring to make short flights through the canopy or brush.

=== Feeding ===
Puerto Rican tanagers feed mainly on invertebrates and fruits.<ref name=":2">{{cite journal|author=Pérez-Rivera, R. A. |year=1997|title= The Importance of vertebrates in the diet of tanagers|journal= Journal of Field Ornithology|volume= 68|issue=|pages= 178–182}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{cite journal|author=Carlo, A. T.|author2= Collazo, A. J.|author3= Groom, J. M. |year=2004 |title=Influences of Fruit Diversity and Abundance in Bird Use of Two Shade Coffee Plantations|journal= Biotropica |volume=36|issue=4|pages= 602–614 |doi= 10.1111/j.1744-7429.2004.tb00354.x|bibcode= 2004Biotr..36..602C|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/227630051}}</ref> The species has been reported to occasionally consume lizards and the nestling of other birds, but most of its diet consists of spiders, insects, centipedes, snails, and various fruits.<ref name=":2" /> Fruit consumption is determined by season, but they often feed on fruits of the genus ''[[Cecropia]]'' and ''[[Clusia]]''.<ref name=":3" /> ''[[Eleutherodactylus]]'' [[tree frog]]s, such as the [[common coquí]], are also an important component of the Puerto Rican tanager's diet.<ref name=":2" />

=== Breeding ===
[[File:Bamboo in Puerto Rico.jpg|thumb|261x261px|Clumps of bamboo in a Puerto Rican subtropical wet forest. Typical roosting habitat for the Puerto Rican tanager.]]
The breeding season lasts from January to late-July, though individuals have been recorded breeding at other times of the year.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> During this time the males become strongly territorial. Nests are located at the ends of branches {{cvt|2–10|m|ft|-1}} off the ground and are usually cup-shaped and approximately {{cvt|9.2|cm}} across. They are typically constructed of vines, ferns, roots, and palm fibers and lined with feathers and palm leaves. Females have a clutch of two or three elliptical white eggs with reddish-brown splotches.<ref name=":1" />

=== Predators ===
Communal roosting makes the species an easy target for owls on the island, such as the [[Puerto Rican owl]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Wetmore, A. |year=1922|title= Bird remains from the caves of Puerto Rico|journal= Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History |volume=46|pages= 297–333}}</ref> They are also a common prey item of the [[Puerto Rican sharp-shinned hawk]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Delannoy, A. C.|author2= Cruz, A. |year=1999|title= Patterns of Prey Abundance and Use by Male and Female Puerto Rican Sharp-shinned Hawks |journal=Caribbean Journal of Science |volume=35|issue=1–2|pages= 38–45}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The Puerto Rican tanager is restricted to mid- to high-elevation ({{cvt|300–1350|m|-2}} montane forests on the island of Puerto Rico.<ref name=":0" /> It typically inhabits mature and [[Secondary forest|second growth]] montane subtropical rain and wet forests, as well as subtropical lower montane forests.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Collazo, A. J.|author2= Bonilla-Martínez, G. I. |year=1988|title= Comparación de la riqueza de aves entre plantaciones de pino hondureño (''Pinus caribaea'') y areas de bosque nativo en el Bosque Estatal de Carite, Cayey, Puerto Rico|journal=Caribbean Journal of Science |volume=24|issue=1–2|pages= 1–10|issn=0008-6452}}</ref> Much of the population is concentrated on the eastern and western sides of the central cordillera of Puerto Rico, with populations in [[El Yunque National Forest|El Yunque]] National Forest and [[Maricao State Forest|Maricao]] State Forest. Deforestation has contributed to fragmentation of the population, which once stretched across the entire central cordillera, but is now confined to the preserved areas and higher peaks.

zh.extract:
暗唐纳雀（台湾作波多黎各唐納雀，学名：Nesospingus speculiferus），是雀形目暗唐纳雀科的唯一一种鸟类。
暗唐纳雀体重约35.2克，翼长约84.5毫米，嘴峰长约21.4毫米，喙宽度约6.6毫米，喙厚度约8毫米，跗蹠长约23毫米，尾长约62毫米。暗唐纳雀在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。
暗唐纳雀分布于波多黎各的高地。该物种是单型物种，没有亚种分化。
